other_side: NLW's throughline is that Anthropic now holds an inherent power — potentially more control over people than any company in history — over who gets to build the tools of the new economy. Writing nine days earlier, Zitron's retort is that there is no measurable economy beneath it to control: you cannot measure the cost of an AI task or its return, and were the ROI real it would be obvious and uncontested. He points to enterprises already pulling back — Walmart capping its internal coding tool, an Amazon executive telling staff not to "use AI just for the sake of using AI." On his telling, Anthropic's "power" is the fragile leverage of an unprofitable vendor over confused buyers, not durable control over a thriving new economy.
truth_beneath: Beneath the heat both men orbit one variable: whether the labs' leverage is real structural power or the brittle pricing power of an unprofitable supplier. They accept the same facts — Anthropic can silently degrade or gate a model and dictate retention terms, yet it reversed a policy within a day under pressure, and enterprises are imposing caps. The empirical question that resolves is whether Anthropic keeps its customers and pricing once it must charge true costs around an IPO; the observable is switching behaviour — Microsoft trimming its Anthropic use, firms swapping in cheaper or Chinese models — versus genuine lock-in after price rises. The residue is worldview: is gatekeeping the future sovereign-level power or salesmanship? NLW is vindicated if buyers stay and the lab becomes a chokepoint; Zitron if they flee to substitutes once subsidies end. Both are polemicists — NLW anti-doom on the labs' significance, Zitron anti-hype and bubble-certain — and both now treat willingness to pay true cost as the decisive test. Watch switching after the next price change.
